competencies.competency_text: Explains the functioning of local institutions (family, school, bank/post office, market, and panchayat) in different forms (story, drawing, tabulating data, reports), and analyses their roles
competencies.justification: The mela functions as a community market-institution. 'At the Mela' introduces and students identify its supporting institutions: Lost and Found booth, Help Booth, First Aid, police, fire engine, ambulance. Write tasks ask students to explain the purpose of the Lost and Found booth and the role of the police dog — direct analysis of institutional roles.

competencies.competency_text: Identifies natural and humanmade systems that support their lives (water supply, water cycle, river flow systems, seasons, life cycle of plants and animals, food, household items, transport, communication, electricity in the home)
competencies.justification: Transport systems (bus, train, autorickshaw) are central to 'On the Way to the Mela' — students observe multiple modes used in a single outing. 'At the Mela' adds civic safety systems (ambulance, police jeep, fire engine, water point, dustbins). Let us reflect Discuss tasks on types of vehicles reinforce identification of transport systems.
sections: On the Way to the Mela

competencies.competency_text: Describes the basic safety needs and protection (health and hygiene, food, water, shelter, precautions, awareness of emergency situations, abuse, and unsafe situations) of humans, birds, and animals
competencies.justification: Safety is architecturally present throughout: road safety when walking to bus stop; bus safety rules (don't lean out); handwashing before eating at the mela; waste disposal in dustbin; emergency vehicles at mela entrance. Let us reflect has explicit discussion of travel safety and care for elders during travel.
sections: On the Way to the Mela

competencies.competency_text: Learns about basic social and behavioural norms, values, and dispositions that benefit our social and natural environments and that help our society function smoothly (using dustbins, standing in queues, conserving water, using public transportation, keeping one's environment clean, always helping others in need regardless of background)
competencies.justification: Multiple explicitly modelled norms: Rohit's father buys tickets and instructs children on bus etiquette; reserved seat for elderly Dadiji; Mohan Chacha gets a wheelchair; handwashing before eating; waste in dustbin after eating. These are narrative models, not incidental mentions — they are the teaching content of the journey and mela sections.
sections: On the Way to the Mela

competencies.competency_text: Explains a line drawing of their school, village, and ward
competencies.justification: Activity 1 ('Reading a Sketch') presents a neighbourhood map showing parade ground, Nita's house, Sneha's house, police station, school, bank, post office, library. Students read and interpret the map to answer four questions about location and route — direct execution of C-5.1.

competencies.competency_text: Draws a sketch of their school, village, and ward using symbols and directions
competencies.justification: The Draw task immediately following Activity 1 instructs students to sketch their own route from home to school — producing a personal route map. Let us reflect Draw task asks students to draw and label different vehicles near school and home, extending spatial representation.

competencies.competency_text: Performs simple inquiry related to specific questions independently or in groups
competencies.justification: Two Find out tasks require independent inquiry: asking elders how melas were different when they were young; finding out why fire engines and ambulances are present at melas. Let us reflect role-play (mock mela) is a group inquiry-and-planning task requiring students to investigate and allocate mela roles.
